From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American multinational technology company

"Dell Inc." redirects here. For its parent company, see [Dell Technologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Technologies "Dell Technologies"). For other uses, see [Dell (disambiguation)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_(disambiguation) "Dell (disambiguation)").

Dell Inc.[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Dell_logo_2016.svg/250px-Dell_logo_2016.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dell_logo_2016.svg)

Logo as of September 7, 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit)
[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/RR1-_Dell_Campus.jpg/330px-RR1-_Dell_Campus.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:RR1-_Dell_Campus.jpg)

Headquarters in Round Rock, Texas
Formerly*   PC's Limited (1984–1987)
*   Dell Computer Corporation (1987–2003)
Company type[Subsidiary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Subsidiary "Subsidiary")
[Traded as](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ticker_symbol "Ticker symbol")[Nasdaq](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nasdaq "Nasdaq"): DELL (until 2013)
Industry*   [Personal computers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer")
*   [Computer software](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software "Software")
Founded May 3, 1984; 41 years ago(1984-05-03) in [Austin, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), U.S.
Founder[Michael Dell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Dell "Michael Dell")
Headquarters[Round Rock, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Round_Rock,_Texas "Round Rock, Texas")

, 

U.S.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-1)
Area served Worldwide
Key people*   Michael Dell ([chairman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chairperson "Chairperson")&[CEO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer"))
*   Jeff Clarke ([vice chair](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chairperson "Chairperson")&[COO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chief_operating_officer "Chief operating officer"))
Products*   [Personal computers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer")
*   [Servers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Server_(computing) "Server (computing)")
*   [Peripheral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peripheral "Peripheral")
Revenue![Image 6: Decrease](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Decrease2.svg/20px-Decrease2.svg.png)US$88.4 billion (2024)
[Operating income](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earnings_before_interest_and_taxes "Earnings before interest and taxes")![Image 7: Decrease](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Decrease2.svg/20px-Decrease2.svg.png) US$5.21 billion (2024)
[Net income](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Net_income "Net income")![Image 8: Increase](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Increase2.svg/20px-Increase2.svg.png) US$3.21 billion (2024)
[Total assets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asset "Asset")![Image 9: Decrease](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Decrease2.svg/20px-Decrease2.svg.png) US$82.1 billion (2024)
[Total equity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equity_(finance) "Equity (finance)")![Image 10: Negative increase](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Increase_Negative.svg/20px-Increase_Negative.svg.png) −US$2.3 billion (2024)
Number of employees c. 120,000[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-2)
[Parent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parent_company "Parent company")[Dell Technologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Technologies "Dell Technologies") (2016–present)
Website[www.dell.com](https://www.dell.com/)[![Image 11: Edit this at Wikidata](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/8a/OOjs_UI_icon_edit-ltr-progressive.svg/20px-OOjs_UI_icon_edit-ltr-progressive.svg.png)](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q30873#P856 "Edit this at Wikidata")

**Dell Inc.**, formerly **Dell Computer Corporation**, is an American [technology company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Technology_company "Technology company") that develops, sells, repairs, and supports [personal computers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") (PCs), [servers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Server_(computing) "Server (computing)"), [data storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_storage "Data storage") devices, [network switches](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_switch "Network switch"), [software](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software "Software"), computer [peripherals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peripheral "Peripheral") including printers and [webcams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Webcam "Webcam") among other products and services. Dell is based in [Round Rock, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Round_Rock,_Texas "Round Rock, Texas").

Founded by [Michael Dell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Dell "Michael Dell") in 1984, Dell started making [IBM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM "IBM")[clone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_PC_compatible "IBM PC compatible") computers and pioneered selling cut-price PCs directly to customers,[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-3) managing its [supply chain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supply_chain_management "Supply chain management") and [electronic commerce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electronic_commerce "Electronic commerce").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-bw1103-4)[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-statesman-5) The company rose rapidly during the 1990s[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-6) and in 2001 became the largest global PC vendor for the first time.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-7) Dell was a pure hardware vendor until 2009 when it acquired [Perot Systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perot_Systems "Perot Systems"). It then entered the market for IT services. The company has expanded storage and networking systems. In the late 2000s, it began expanding from offering computers only to delivering a range of technology for enterprise customers.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-reut-8)

Dell is a subsidiary of [Dell Technologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Technologies "Dell Technologies"), a [publicly traded company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_company "Public company"), as well as a component of the [NASDAQ-100](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NASDAQ-100 "NASDAQ-100") and [S&P 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S%26P_500 "S&P 500"). The company is ranked 31st on the Fortune 500 list in 2022,[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-9) up from 76th in 2021.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-10) It is also the sixth-largest company in Texas by total revenue, according to _Fortune_ magazine. Dell is the second-largest non-oil company in Texas.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-11)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-12) As of 2025,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit) it is the [world's third-largest personal computer vendor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Market_share_of_personal_computer_vendors "Market share of personal computer vendors") by unit sales, after [Lenovo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo") and [HP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HP_Inc. "HP Inc."). In 2015, Dell acquired the enterprise technology firm [EMC Corporation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EMC_Corporation "EMC Corporation"), together becoming divisions of Dell Technologies. Dell began marketing data storage, information security, [virtualization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virtualization "Virtualization"), analytics, and [cloud computing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing") products under the brand Dell EMC until around 2020.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-13)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![Image 12](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Michael_Dell_2010.jpg/250px-Michael_Dell_2010.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Michael_Dell_2010.jpg)

[Michael Dell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Dell "Michael Dell") (founder)

[![Image 13: Dell's first logo from May 3, 1987 to March 1, 1992](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Dell_1984.svg/250px-Dell_1984.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dell_1984.svg)

Dell's first logo from May 3, 1987 to March 1, 1992

[![Image 14: Dell's former logo, used from March 1, 1992 to November 23, 2010 as primary and from November 23, 2010 to September 7, 2016 as secondary](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Dell_logo.svg/250px-Dell_logo.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dell_logo.svg)

Dell's former logo, used from March 1, 1992 to November 23, 2010 as primary and from November 23, 2010 to September 7, 2016 as secondary

[![Image 15: Dell's logo used before the acquisition of EMC, used from November 23, 2010 to September 7, 2016](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Dell_Logo.svg/250px-Dell_Logo.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dell_Logo.svg)

Dell's logo used before the acquisition of EMC, used from November 23, 2010 to September 7, 2016

### Founding and start-up

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding and start-up")]

[![Image 16](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/PC%27s_Limited_Turbo_PC.jpg/250px-PC%27s_Limited_Turbo_PC.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:PC%27s_Limited_Turbo_PC.jpg)

The first PC model, manufactured by Dell (known as PC's Limited at the time), the Turbo PC

[Michael Dell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Dell "Michael Dell") founded Dell Computer Corporation, doing business as PC's Limited, in 1984. Dell was a student at the [University of Texas at Austin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin"),[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-15) and operated the business from his off-campus dormitory room at [Dobie Center](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dobie_Center "Dobie Center").[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-16) The start-up aimed to sell [IBM PC compatible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_PC_compatible "IBM PC compatible") computers built from stock components. Michael Dell started trading in the belief that, by selling personal computer systems directly to customers, PC's Limited could better understand customers' needs and provide the most effective computing services to meet those needs.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-17) Dell dropped out of college upon completion of his freshman year at the University of Texas in order to focus full-time on his fledgling business, after getting about $1,000 in expansion-capital from his family.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-delltimeline-18) As of April 2021, Dell's net worth was estimated to be over $50 billion (equivalent to $56,940,000,000 in 2024).[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-19)

In 1985, PC's Limited launched its first computer, the "Turbo PC", priced at US$795 (equivalent to $1,963 in 2024).[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-20) The Turbo PC featured an Intel 8088-compatible processor with a maximum speed of 8 MHz.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-21) At that time PC's Limited was considered one of many [white box](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/White_box_(computer_hardware) "White box (computer hardware)") vendors, although by 1986 [Hughes Aircraft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hughes_Aircraft "Hughes Aircraft") was evaluating its products for corporate use after an executive's positive experience with a personal purchase.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-welch19860127-22) The company marketed these systems through national computer magazines, selling directly to consumers while custom-assembling each unit based on a range of options. This approach allowed PC's Limited to offer competitive prices compared to retail brands, coupled with the convenience of pre-assembled units, making them one of the early success stories of this business model. The company grossed over $73 million in its first year of operation. The company dropped the _PC's Limited_ name in 1987 to become Dell Computer Corporation and began expanding globally. The reasoning was that this new company name better reflected its presence in the business market, and also resolved issues with the use of "Limited" in a company name in certain countries.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ferrell198708-23) The company set up its first international operations in Britain; 11 more followed within the next four years. In June 1988, Dell Computer's market capitalization grew by $30 million to $85 million ($193.9 million in 2025) from its June 22 initial public offering of 3.5 million shares at $8.50 a share on [NASDAQ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NASDAQ "NASDAQ") under the ticker symbol DELL.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-24) In 1989, the company launched its first laptop product, the Dell 316LT.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-25)

### Growth in the 1990s and early 2000s

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Growth in the 1990s and early 2000s")]

[![Image 17](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Dell_Latitude_CPx.jpg/250px-Dell_Latitude_CPx.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dell_Latitude_CPx.jpg)

Dell Latitude CPx laptop

In 1990, Dell Computer tried selling its products indirectly through warehouse clubs and computer superstores, but met with little success, and the company re-focused on its more successful direct-to-consumer sales model. In 1992, _[Fortune](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fortune\_(magazine) "Fortune (magazine)")_ included Dell Computer Corporation in its list of the world's [500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fortune_Global_500 "Fortune Global 500") largest companies, making Michael Dell the youngest CEO of a Fortune 500 company at that time.

Senior vice president [Joel Kocher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joel_Kocher "Joel Kocher") told _[The Wall Street Journal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Wall\_Street\_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")_ in 1993 "this isn't a technology business anymore". His view, that PCs were commodities, was reportedly widely held by others in the company. They thought that Dell differentiated itself from others—like fellow Texas company and archrival [Compaq](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compaq "Compaq")—with its distribution expertise and "database engine" of customers that, Kocher said, could sell anything including non-technology products: "We're more like [Mary Kay Cosmetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mary_Kay_Cosmetics "Mary Kay Cosmetics") than we are like [General Motors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors")".[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-pope19930702-26)

In 1993, to complement its own direct sales channel, Dell planned to sell PCs at big-box retail outlets such as [Wal-Mart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wal-Mart "Wal-Mart"), which would have brought in an additional $125 million (equivalent to $244,400,000 in 2024) in annual revenue. [Bain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bain_Capital "Bain Capital") consultant [Kevin Rollins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kevin_Rollins "Kevin Rollins") persuaded Michael Dell to pull out of these deals, believing they would be money losers in the long run.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-27) Margins at retail were thin at best and Dell left the reseller channel in 1994.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-mhhe.com-28) Rollins would soon join Dell full-time and eventually become the company president and CEO.

By the early 1990s the [laptop computer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laptop_computer "Laptop computer") market was both more profitable and faster-growing than the overall personal computer market. After discontinuing its unsuccessful existing products in 1993, and hiring John Medica—who had led development of the very successful Apple [PowerBook](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PowerBook "PowerBook")—the company in 1994 introduced the [Dell Latitude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Latitude "Dell Latitude") laptop line.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-lohr19940222-29)

Originally, Dell did not emphasize the consumer market, due to the higher costs and low profit margins in selling to individuals and households; this changed when the company's Internet site took off in 1996 and 1997.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-delltimeline-18) While the industry's average selling price to individuals was going down, Dell's was going up, as second- and third-time computer buyers who wanted powerful computers with multiple features and did not need much technical support were choosing Dell. Dell found an opportunity among PC-savvy individuals who liked the convenience of buying direct, customizing their PC to their means, and having it delivered in days. In early 1997, Dell created an internal sales and marketing group dedicated to serving the home market and introduced a product line designed especially for individual users.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-mhhe.com-28)

Dell's growth in the 1990s[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-30)| Year | Revenue (mill. US$) | No. of employees |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1990 | 546 | 2,050 |
| 1991 | 889 | 2,970 |
| 1992 | 2,013 | 4,650 |
| 1993 | 2,873 | 5,980 |
| 1994 | 3,475 | 6,400 |
| 1995 | 5,296 | 8,400 |
| 1996 | 7,759 | 10,350 |
| 1997 | 12,327 | 16,000 |
| 1998 | 18,243 | 24,400 |
| 1999 | 25,256 | 36,500 |

From 1997 to 2004, Dell steadily grew and it gained market share from competitors even during industry slumps, with its fastest growth occurring in the early 2000s. During the same period, rival PC vendors such as [Compaq](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compaq "Compaq"), [Gateway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gateway,_Inc. "Gateway, Inc."), [IBM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_Aptiva "IBM Aptiva"), [Packard Bell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Packard_Bell "Packard Bell"), and [AST Research](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AST_Research "AST Research") struggled and eventually left the market or were bought out.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ZDA-31) Dell surpassed Compaq to become the largest PC manufacturer in 1999.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-32) Operating costs made up only 10 percent of Dell's $35 billion in revenue in 2002 (equivalent to $58,170,000,000 in 2024), compared with 21 percent of revenue at Hewlett-Packard, 25 percent at Gateway, and 46 percent at Cisco.[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-33) In 2002, when Compaq merged with Hewlett-Packard (the fourth-place PC maker), the newly combined Hewlett-Packard took the top spot for a time but struggled and Dell soon regained its lead.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-bw1103-4)

In 2002, Dell expanded its product line to include televisions, [handhelds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Handhelds "Handhelds"), digital audio players, and [printers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Printer_(computing) "Printer (computing)"). Chairman and CEO Michael Dell had repeatedly blocked President and COO Kevin Rollins's attempt to lessen the company's heavy dependency on PCs, which Rollins wanted to fix by acquiring EMC Corporation; a move that would eventually occur over 12 years later.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-CNNMoney-dilemma-34)

In 2003, at the annual company meeting, the stockholders approved changing the company name to "Dell Inc." to recognize the company's expansion beyond computers.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-May-2003-PRE-14A-35)

In 2004, the company announced that it would build a new assembly-plant near [Winston-Salem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Winston-Salem,_North_Carolina "Winston-Salem, North Carolina"), [North Carolina](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina"); the city and county provided Dell with $37.2 million in incentive packages; the state provided approximately $250 million (equivalent to $396,700,000 in 2024) in incentives and tax breaks. In July, Michael Dell stepped aside as [chief executive officer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") while retaining his position as [chairman of the board](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chair_(official) "Chair (official)").[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-May-2004-DEF-14A-36) Kevin Rollins, who had held a number of executive posts at Dell, became the new CEO. Despite no longer holding the CEO title, Dell essentially acted as a de facto co-CEO with Rollins.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-CNNMoney-dilemma-34)

Under Rollins, Dell purchased the computer hardware manufacturer [Alienware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alienware "Alienware") in 2006. Dell Inc.'s plan anticipated Alienware continuing to operate independently under its existing management. Alienware expected to benefit from Dell's efficient manufacturing system.[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-37)

### Struggles in mid-2000s

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Struggles in mid-2000s")]

[![Image 18](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/DELL_AIXM_X51v.jpg/250px-DELL_AIXM_X51v.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:DELL_AIXM_X51v.jpg)

Dell Axim X51v, shown with the Japanese Wikipedia main page open

In 2005, while earnings and sales continued to rise, sales growth slowed considerably, and the company stock lost 25% of its value that year.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-BW0206-38) By June 2006, the stock traded around US$25 which was 40% down from July 2005—the high-water mark of the company in the post-dotcom era.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-nytimes2006-39)[[40]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-40)

The slowing sales growth has been attributed to the maturing PC market, which constituted 66% of Dell's sales, and analysts suggested that Dell needed to make inroads into non-PC business segments such as storage, services, and servers. Dell's price advantage was tied to its ultra-lean manufacturing for desktop PCs,[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-autogenerated1-41) but this became less important as savings became harder to find inside the company's supply chain, and as competitors such as Hewlett-Packard and [Acer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acer_Inc. "Acer Inc.") made their PC manufacturing operations more efficient to match Dell, weakening Dell's traditional price differentiation.[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-news.cnet.com-42) Throughout the entire PC industry, declines in prices along with commensurate increases in performance meant that Dell had fewer opportunities to upsell to their customers. As a result, the company was selling a greater proportion of inexpensive PCs than before, which eroded profit margins.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ZDA-31) The laptop segment had become the fastest-growing of the PC market, but Dell produced low-cost notebooks in China like other PC manufacturers which eliminated Dell's manufacturing cost advantages, plus Dell's reliance on Internet sales meant that it missed out on growing notebook sales in big box stores.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-nytimes2006-39)_CNET_ has suggested that Dell was getting trapped in the increasing commoditization of high volume low margin computers, which prevented it from offering more exciting devices that consumers demanded.[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-autogenerated1-41)

Despite plans of expanding into other global regions and product segments, Dell was heavily dependent on US corporate PC market, as desktop PCs sold to both commercial and corporate customers accounted for 32 percent of its revenue, 85 percent of its revenue comes from businesses, and 64 percent of its revenue comes from North and South America, according to its 2006 third-quarter results. US shipments of desktop PCs were shrinking, and the corporate PC market, which purchases PCs in upgrade cycles, had largely decided to take a break from buying new systems. The last cycle started around 2002, three or so years after companies started buying PCs ahead of the perceived [Y2K](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Year_2000_problem "Year 2000 problem") problems, and corporate clients were not expected to upgrade again until extensive testing of Microsoft's [Windows Vista](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_Vista "Windows Vista") (expected in early 2007), putting the next upgrade cycle around 2008.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ReferenceA-43)[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ReferenceB-44) Heavily dependent on PCs, Dell had to slash prices to boost sales volumes, while demanding deep cuts from suppliers.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-CNNMoney-dilemma-34)

Dell had long stuck by its direct sales model. Consumers had become the main drivers of PC sales in recent years,[[44]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ReferenceB-44) yet there had a decline in consumers purchasing PCs through the Web or on the phone, as increasing numbers were visiting consumer electronics retail stores to try out the devices first. Dell's rivals in the PC industry, HP, Gateway and [Acer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acer_Inc. "Acer Inc."), had a long retail presence and so were well poised to take advantage of the consumer shift.[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-director1-45) The lack of a retail presence stymied Dell's attempts to offer [consumer electronics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics") such as flat-panel TVs and MP3 players.[[41]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-autogenerated1-41) Dell responded by experimenting with mall kiosks, plus quasi-retail stores in Texas and New York.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ReferenceA-43)

Dell had a reputation as a company that relied upon supply chain efficiencies to sell established technologies at low prices, instead of being an innovator.[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-CNNMoney-dilemma-34)[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-director1-45)[[46]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-46) By the mid-2000s many analysts were looking to innovating companies as the next source of growth in the technology sector. Dell's low spending on R&D relative to its revenue (compared to [IBM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM "IBM"), [Hewlett-Packard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard"), and [Apple Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."))—which worked well in the commoditized PC market—prevented it from making inroads into more lucrative segments, such as MP3 players and later mobile devices.[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-BW0206-38) Increasing spending on R&D would have cut into the operating margins that the company emphasized.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-bw1103-4) Dell had done well with a horizontal organization that focused on PCs when the computing industry moved to horizontal mix-and-match layers in the 1980s, but by the mid-2000 the industry shifted to vertically integrated stacks to deliver an end-to-end IT product, and Dell lagged far behind competitors like Hewlett-Packard and Oracle.[[42]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-news.cnet.com-42)

In 2006, Dell rolled out _DellConnect_ to answer customer inquiries more quickly. In July 2006, the company started its Direct2Dell blog, and then in February 2007, Michael Dell launched IdeaStorm.com, asking customers for advice including selling Linux computers and reducing the promotional "bloatware" on PCs. These initiatives did manage to cut the negative blog posts from 49% to 22%, as well as reduce the "Dell Hell" prominent on Internet search engines.[[39]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-nytimes2006-39)[[47]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-47)

There was also criticism that Dell used faulty components for its PCs, particularly the 11.8 million OptiPlex desktop computers sold to businesses and governments from May 2003 to July 2005 that suffered from [faulty capacitors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Capacitor_plague "Capacitor plague").[[48]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-48) A battery recall in August 2006, as a result of a Dell laptop catching fire, caused much negative attention for the company though later, [Sony](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sony "Sony") was found responsible for the manufacturing of the batteries, however a Sony spokesman said the problem concerned the combination of the battery with a charger, which was specific to Dell.[[49]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-49)

2006 marked the first year that Dell's growth was slower than the PC industry as a whole. By the fourth quarter of 2006, Dell lost its title of the largest PC manufacturer to Hewlett Packard whose Personal Systems Group was invigorated thanks to a restructuring initiated by their CEO [Mark Hurd](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mark_Hurd "Mark Hurd").[[38]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-BW0206-38)[[50]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-50)[[51]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-51)

#### SEC investigation

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: SEC investigation")]

In August 2005, Dell became the subject of an informal investigation by the United States [SEC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission").[[52]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-52) In 2006, the company disclosed that the US Attorney for the Southern District of New York had subpoenaed documents related to the company's financial reporting dating back to 2002.[[53]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-53) The company delayed filing financial reports for the third and fourth fiscal quarter of 2006, and several class-action lawsuits were filed.[[54]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-54) Dell Inc's failure to file its quarterly earnings report could have subjected the company to de-listing from the [Nasdaq](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nasdaq "Nasdaq"),[[55]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-55) but the exchange granted Dell a waiver, allowing the stock to trade normally.[[56]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-56) In August 2007, the company announced that it would restate its earnings for fiscal years 2003 through 2006 and the first quarter of 2007 after an internal audit found that certain employees had changed corporate account balances to meet quarterly financial targets.[[57]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-57) In July 2010, the SEC announced charges against several senior Dell executives, including Dell Chairman and CEO Michael Dell, former CEO Kevin Rollins, and former CFO James Schneider, "with failing to disclose material information to investors and using fraudulent accounting to make it falsely appear that the company was consistently meeting Wall Street earnings targets and reducing its operating expenses." Dell, inc. was fined $100 million, with Michael Dell personally fined $4 million.[[58]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-58)

### Dell 2.0 and downsizing

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Dell 2.0 and downsizing")]

After four out of five quarterly earnings reports were below expectations, Rollins resigned as president and CEO on January 31, 2007, and founder Michael Dell assumed the role of CEO again.[[59]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-Feb-2007-8-K-59)

On March 1, 2007, the company issued a preliminary quarterly earnings report showing gross sales of $14.4 billion, down 5% year-over-year, and net income of $687 million (30 cents per share), down 33%. Net earnings would have declined even more if not for the effects of eliminated employee bonuses, which accounted for six cents per share. NASDAQ extended the company's deadline for filing financials to May 4.[[60]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-60)

Dell announced a change campaign called "Dell 2.0," reducing the number of employees and diversifying the company's products.[[45]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-director1-45)[[61]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-61) While chairman of the board after relinquishing his CEO position, Michael Dell still had significant input in the company during Rollins' years as CEO. With the return of Michael Dell as CEO, the company saw changes in operations, the exodus of many senior vice-presidents and new personnel brought in from outside the company.[[43]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ReferenceA-43) Michael Dell announced a number of initiatives and plans (part of the "Dell 2.0" initiative) to improve the company's financial performance. These include elimination of 2006 bonuses for employees with some discretionary awards, reduction in the number of managers reporting directly to Michael Dell from 20 to 12, and reduction of "[bureaucracy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bureaucracy "Bureaucracy")". Jim Schneider retired as CFO and was replaced by [Donald Carty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Donald_Carty "Donald Carty"), as the company came under an SEC probe for its accounting practices.[[62]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-62)

On April 23, 2008, Dell announced the closure of one of its biggest Canadian call-centers in [Kanata, Ontario](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kanata,_Ontario "Kanata, Ontario"), terminating approximately 1100 employees, with 500 of those redundancies effective on the spot, and with the official closure of the center scheduled for the summer. The call-center had opened in 2006 after the city of [Ottawa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa") won a bid to host it. Less than a year later, Dell planned to double its workforce to nearly 3,000 workers add a new building. These plans were reversed, due to a high [Canadian dollar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar") that made the Ottawa staff relatively expensive, and also as part of Dell's turnaround, which involved moving these call-center jobs offshore to cut costs. [[63]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-63) The company had also announced the shutdown of its [Edmonton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), [Alberta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), office, losing 900 jobs. In total, Dell announced the ending of about 8,800 jobs in 2007–2008— 10% of its workforce.[[64]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-64)

By the late 2000s, Dell's "configure to order" approach of manufacturing—delivering individual PCs configured to customer specifications from its US facilities was no longer as efficient or competitive with high-volume Asian contract manufacturers as PCs became powerful low-cost commodities.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-statesman-5)[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-statesman1-65) Dell closed plants that produced desktop computers for the North American market, including the Mort Topfer Manufacturing Center in [Austin, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") (original location)[[66]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-66)[[67]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-67) and [Lebanon, Tennessee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lebanon,_Tennessee "Lebanon, Tennessee") (opened in 1999) in 2008 and early 2009, respectively. The desktop production plant in [Winston-Salem, North Carolina](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Winston-Salem,_North_Carolina "Winston-Salem, North Carolina"), received [US$](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/US$ "US$")280 million in incentives from the state and opened in 2005 (equivalent to $430,900,000 in 2024), but ceased operations in November 2010. Dell's contract with the state required them to repay the incentives for failing to meet the conditions, and they sold the North Carolina plant to Herbalife.[[68]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-68)[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-theregister.co.uk-69)[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-bizjournals.com-70) Much work was transferred to manufacturers in Asia and Mexico, or some of Dell's own factories overseas.[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-statesman1-65) On January 8, 2009, Dell announced the closure of its manufacturing plant in Limerick, Ireland, with the loss of 1,900 jobs and the transfer of production to its plant in [Łodź](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C5%81od%C5%BA "Łodź") in Poland.[[71]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-71)

### Attempts at diversification

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Attempts at diversification")]

[![Image 19](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/IFA_2010_Internationale_Funkausstellung_Berlin_57.JPG/250px-IFA_2010_Internationale_Funkausstellung_Berlin_57.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:IFA_2010_Internationale_Funkausstellung_Berlin_57.JPG)

Dell Streak smartphone

The release of Apple's [iPad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IPad "IPad")[tablet computer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer") had a negative impact on Dell and other major PC vendors, as consumers switched away from desktop and laptop PCs. Dell's own mobility division has not managed success with developing smartphones or tablets, whether running Windows or [Android](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)").[[72]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-72)[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-beta.fool-73) The [Dell Streak](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Streak "Dell Streak") was a failure commercially and critically due to its outdated OS, numerous bugs, and low resolution screen. _InfoWorld_ suggested that Dell and other OEMs saw tablets as a short-term, low-investment opportunity running [Google Android](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google_Android "Google Android"), an approach that neglected user interface and failed to gain long term market traction with consumers.[[74]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-74)[[75]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-75) Dell has responded by pushing higher-end PCs, such as the XPS line of notebooks, which do not compete with the [Apple iPad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_iPad "Apple iPad") and [Kindle Fire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kindle_Fire "Kindle Fire") tablets.[[76]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-76) The growing popularity of smartphones and tablet computers instead of PCs drove Dell's consumer segment to an operating loss in Q3 2012. In December 2012, Dell suffered its first decline in holiday sales in five years, despite the introduction of [Windows 8](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_8 "Windows 8").[[77]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-77)

In the shrinking PC industry, Dell continued to lose market share, as it dropped below [Lenovo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo") in 2011 to fall to number three in the world. Dell and fellow American contemporary Hewlett Packard came under pressure from Asian PC manufacturers Lenovo, [Asus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asus "Asus"), and Acer, all of which had lower production costs and were willing to accept lower profit margins. In addition, while the Asian PC vendors had been improving their quality and design—for instance, Lenovo's [ThinkPad](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ThinkPad "ThinkPad") series was winning corporate customers away from Dell's laptops—Dell's customer service and reputation had been slipping.[[78]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-78)[[79]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-79) Dell remained the second-most profitable PC vendor, as it took 13 percent of operating profits in the PC industry during Q4 2012, behind Apple's Mac that took 45 percent, seven percent at Hewlett Packard, six percent at Lenovo and Asus, and one percent for Acer.[[80]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-80)

Dell attempted to offset its declining PC business, which still accounted for half of its revenue and generates steady cash flow,[[81]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-81) by expanding into the enterprise market with servers, networking, software, and services.[[82]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-82) It avoided many of the acquisition write-downs and management turnover that plagued its chief rival Hewlett Packard.[[73]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-beta.fool-73)[[83]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-chron.com-83) Despite spending $13 billion on acquisitions to diversify its portfolio beyond hardware,[[84]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-bloomberg.com-84) the company was unable to convince the market that it could thrive or made the transformation in the post-PC world,[[83]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-chron.com-83) as it suffered continued declines in revenue and share price.[[85]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-85)[[86]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-86)[[87]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-87)[[88]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-economist-88) Dell's market share in the corporate segment was previously a "moat" against rivals but this has no longer been the case as sales and profits have fallen precipitously.[[89]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-89)

### 2013 buyout

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2013 buyout")]

After several weeks of rumors, which started around January 11, 2013, Dell announced on February 5, 2013, that it had struck a $24.4 billion (equivalent to $32,310,000,000 in 2024) [leveraged buyout](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leveraged_buyout "Leveraged buyout") deal, that would have delisted its shares from the NASDAQ and Hong Kong Stock Exchange and taken it private.[[90]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-90)[[91]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-91)[[92]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-92)[Reuters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reuters "Reuters") reported that Michael Dell and [Silver Lake Partners](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silver_Lake_Partners "Silver Lake Partners"), aided by a $2 billion loan from [Microsoft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), would acquire the public shares at $13.65 apiece.[[93]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-buyout-93) The $24.4 billion buyout was projected to be the largest leveraged buyout backed by private equity since the [2008 financial crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis") (equivalent to $35,460,000,000 in 2024).[[94]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-buyout2-94) It is also the largest technology buyout ever, surpassing the 2006 buyout of [Freescale Semiconductor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Freescale_Semiconductor "Freescale Semiconductor") for $17.5 billion (equivalent to $26,120,000,000 in 2024).[[94]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-buyout2-94)

Michael Dell said of the February offer "I believe this transaction will open an exciting new chapter for Dell, our customers and team members".[[95]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-buyout3-95) Dell rival Lenovo responded to the buyout, saying, "the financial actions of some of our traditional competitors will not substantially change our outlook."[[95]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-buyout3-95)

In March 2013, the [Blackstone Group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blackstone_Group "Blackstone Group") and [Carl Icahn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carl_Icahn "Carl Icahn") expressed interest in purchasing Dell.[[96]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-96) In April 2013, Blackstone withdrew their offer, citing deteriorating business.[[97]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-97)[[98]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-nyt-98) Other private equity firms such as [KKR & Co.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KKR_%26_Co. "KKR & Co.") and TPG Capital declined to submit alternative bids for Dell, citing the uncertain market for personal computers and competitive pressures, so the "wide-open bidding war" never materialized.[[84]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-bloomberg.com-84) Analysts said that the biggest challenge facing Silver Lake would be to find an "exit strategy" to profit from its investment, which would be when the company would hold an IPO to go public again, and one warned "But even if you can get a $25bn enterprise value for Dell, it will take years to get out."[[99]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Gelles-99)

In May 2013, Michael Dell joined his board in voting for the offer.[[100]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-100) The following August he reached a deal with the special committee on the board for $13.88 per share, a raised price of $13.75 plus a special dividend of 13 cents, as well as a change to the voting rules.[[101]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-101) The $13.88 cash offer (plus a $.08 per share dividend for the third fiscal quarter) was accepted on September 12[[102]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-102) and closed on October 30, 2013, ending Dell's 25-year run as a publicly traded company.

After the buyout, the newly private Dell offered a Voluntary Separation Program that they expected to reduce their workforce by up to seven percent. The reception to the program so exceeded the expectations that Dell may be forced to hire new staff to make up for the losses.[[103]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-103)

### Acquisition of EMC

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Acquisition of EMC")]

On November 19, 2015, Dell, alongside [Arm Holdings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arm_Holdings "Arm Holdings"), [Cisco Systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cisco "Cisco"), [Intel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intel "Intel"), [Microsoft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), and [Princeton University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"), founded the [OpenFog Consortium](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenFog_Consortium "OpenFog Consortium"), to promote interests and development in [fog computing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fog_computing "Fog computing").[[104]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-104)

On October 12, 2015, Dell Inc. announced its intent to acquire EMC Corporation in a cash-and-stock deal valued at $67 billion (equivalent to $86,390,000,000 in 2024), which has been considered the largest-ever acquisition in the technology sector.[[105]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-105)[[106]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-bbc-dellemc-106) As part of the acquisition, Dell took over EMC's 81% stake in the cloud-computing and virtualization company [VMware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VMware "VMware").[[107]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-EMC-107) This combined Dell's enterprise server, personal computer, and mobile businesses with EMC's enterprise storage business in a significant Vertical merger of IT giants. Dell paid $24.05 per share of EMC, and $9.05 per share of [tracking stock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tracking_stock "Tracking stock") in [VMware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VMware "VMware").[[108]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-wsj-emcdell-108)[[109]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-nyt-emcdell-109)[[106]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-bbc-dellemc-106)

The announcement came two years after Dell Inc. returned to private ownership, claiming that it faced bleak prospects and would need several years out of the public eye to rebuild its business.[[110]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-WSJ-20130329-110) It was thought that the company's value had roughly doubled since then.[[111]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-WSJ-2015-10-12-111) EMC was being pressured by [Elliott Management](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elliott_Management_Corporation "Elliott Management Corporation"), a hedge fund holding 2.2% of EMC's stock, to reorganize their unusual "Federation" structure, in which EMC's divisions were effectively being run as independent companies. Elliott argued[[112]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-BusinessWire-2014-10-08-112) this structure deeply undervalued EMC's core "EMC II" data storage business, and that increasing competition between EMC II and VMware products was confusing the market and hindering both companies. _[The Wall Street Journal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Wall\_Street\_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")_ estimated that in 2014 Dell had revenue of $27.3 billion (equivalent to $35,530,000,000 in 2024) from personal computers and $8.9 billion from servers, while EMC had $16.5 billion from EMC II, $1 billion from [RSA Security](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RSA_Security "RSA Security"), $6 billion from VMware, and $230 million from [Pivotal Software](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pivotal_Software "Pivotal Software").[[113]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-WSJ-2015-10-13-113) EMC owned around 80 percent of the stock of VMware.[[114]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-FT-Lex-2015-10-12-114) The proposed acquisition maintained VMware as a separate company, held via a new [tracking stock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tracking_stock "Tracking stock"), while the other parts of EMC rolled into Dell.[[115]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-FT-2015-10-12-115) Once the acquisition closed, Dell began publishing quarterly financial results again, having ceased these after going private in 2013.[[116]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-116)

The combined business was expected to address the markets for [scale-out architecture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Converged_storage#Scale-out_architecture "Converged storage"), [converged infrastructure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Converged_infrastructure "Converged infrastructure") and [private cloud computing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cloud_computing#Private_cloud "Cloud computing"), playing to the strengths of both EMC and Dell.[[113]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-WSJ-2015-10-13-113)[[117]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Economist-2015-10-12-117) Commentators questioned the deal, with [FBR Capital Markets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FBR_Capital_Markets "FBR Capital Markets") saying that though it makes a "ton of sense" for Dell, it was a "nightmare scenario that would lack strategic synergies" for EMC.[[118]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Reuters-2015-10-08-118)_Fortune_ said there was a lot for Dell to like in EMC's portfolio, but "does it all add up enough to justify tens of billions of dollars for the entire package? Probably not."[[119]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Fortune-2015-10-08-119)_[The Register](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Register "The Register")_ reported the view of [William Blair & Company](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Blair_%26_Company "William Blair & Company") that the merger would "blow up the current IT chess board", forcing other IT infrastructure vendors to restructure to achieve scale and vertical integration.[[120]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Register-2015-10-09-120) The value of VMware stock fell 10% after the announcement, valuing the deal at around $63–64bn rather than the $67bn originally reported.[[121]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-FT-Lex-Live-2015-10-13-121) Key investors backing the deal besides Dell were Singapore's [Temasek Holdings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Temasek_Holdings "Temasek Holdings") and [Silver Lake Partners](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silver_Lake_Partners "Silver Lake Partners").[[122]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-122)

On September 7, 2016, Dell completed the merger with EMC, which involved the issuance of $45.9 billion (equivalent to $58,630,000,000 in 2024) in debt and $4.4 billion (equivalent to $5,620,000,000 in 2024) of common stock.[[123]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-123)[[124]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-124) At the time, some analysts claimed that Dell's acquisition of the former Iomega could harm the [LenovoEMC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LenovoEMC "LenovoEMC") partnership.[[125]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-IomegaPart.TheRegister-125)

### Going public as Dell Technologies

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Going public as Dell Technologies")]

In July 2018, Dell announced intentions to become a publicly traded company again by paying $21.7 billion (equivalent to $26,620,000,000 in 2024) in both cash and stock to buy back shares from its stake in VMware, offering shareholders roughly 60 cents on the dollar as part of the deal.[[126]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-126)[[107]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-EMC-107) In November, Carl Icahn (9.3% owner of Dell) sued the company over plans to go public.[[127]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-127) As a result of pressure from Icahn and other [activist investors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Activist_shareholder "Activist shareholder"), Dell renegotiated the deal, ultimately offering shareholders about 80% of market value. As part of this deal, Dell once again became a public company, with the original Dell computer business and Dell EMC operating under the newly created parent, [Dell Technologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Technologies "Dell Technologies").[[107]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-EMC-107)

Post-acquisition, Dell was re-organized with a new parent company, Dell Technologies, and into three main business divisions: Client Solutions Group, Infrastructure Solutions Group and [VMware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VMware "VMware").[[128]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-128)[[129]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-129)[[130]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-wsjemcdell-130)

In January 2021, Dell reported $94 billion (equivalent to $107,040,000,000 in 2024) in sales and $13 billion (equivalent to $14,800,000,000 in 2024) operating cash flow during 2020.[[107]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-EMC-107)

By June 2021, Dell Technologies' stock price had reached an all-time high of over US$100 per share, reflecting the company's successful transition to a technology service provider that helps customers navigate digital transformation.[[131]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Yahoo_Finance-131)

On March 1, 2024, Dell Technologies' stock hit all-time high after earnings. It delivered a strong performance from its artificial intelligence unit that sent shares up nearly 40%, its highest daily gain since the company went public in 2018.[[132]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-132)

In August 2024, the company announced it would be laying off 12,500 employees—10% of its workforce—in order to invest in artificial intelligence initiatives.[[133]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-133)

### Dell and AMD

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Dell and AMD")]

When Dell acquired Alienware early in 2006, some Alienware systems had [AMD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AMD "AMD") chips. On August 17, 2006, a Dell press release[[134]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-134) stated that starting in September, Dell Dimension desktop computers would have AMD processors and that later in the year Dell would release a two-socket, quad-processor server using AMD [Opteron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opteron "Opteron") chips, moving away from Dell's tradition of only offering Intel processors in Dell PCs.

[CNET](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CNET "CNET")'s News.com on August 17, 2006, cited Dell's CEO Kevin Rollins as attributing the move to AMD processors to lower costs and to AMD technology.[[135]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-135) AMD's senior VP in commercial business, Marty Seyer, stated: "Dell's wider embrace of AMD processor-based offerings is a win for Dell, for the industry and most importantly for Dell customers."

On October 23, 2006, Dell announced new AMD-based servers—the PowerEdge 6950 and the [PowerEdge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PowerEdge "PowerEdge") SC1435.

On November 1, 2006, Dell's website began offering notebooks based on AMD processors (the Inspiron 1501 with a 15.4-inch (390 mm) display) with the choice of a single-core MK-36 processor, dual-core Turion X2 chips or Mobile Sempron.[[136]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-136)

In 2017, Dell released the AlienWare 17 gaming laptop. The model was primarily based on NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 systems.[[137]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-137)

### Dell and desktop Linux

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Dell and desktop Linux")]

In 1998, [Ralph Nader](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ralph_Nader "Ralph Nader") asked Dell (and five other major [OEMs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Original_equipment_manufacturer "Original equipment manufacturer")) to offer alternate operating systems to [Microsoft Windows](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"), specifically including [Linux](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux "Linux"), for which "there is clearly a growing interest".[[138]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-138)[[139]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-139) Possibly coincidentally, Dell started offering Linux notebook systems that "cost no more than their Windows 98 counterparts" in 2000,[[140]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-140) and soon expanded, with Dell becoming "the first major manufacturer to offer Linux across its full product line".[[141]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-141) However, by early 2001 Dell had "disbanded its Linux business unit."[[142]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-142)

On February 26, 2007, Dell announced that it had commenced a program to sell and distribute a range of computers with pre-installed Linux distributions as an alternative to [Microsoft Windows](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"). Dell indicated that [Novell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Novell "Novell")'s [SUSE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SUSE_Linux "SUSE Linux") Linux would appear first.[[143]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-143) However, the next day, Dell announced that its previous announcement related to certifying the hardware as ready to work with Novell SUSE Linux and that it (Dell) had no plans to sell systems pre-installed with Linux in the near future.[[144]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-144) On March 28, 2007, Dell announced that it would begin shipping some desktops and laptops with Linux pre-installed, although it did not specify which distribution of Linux or which hardware would lead.[[145]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-145) On April 18, a report appeared suggesting that Michael Dell used [Ubuntu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubuntu "Ubuntu") on one of his home systems.[[146]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-146) On May 1, 2007, Dell announced it would ship the Ubuntu Linux distribution.[[147]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-147) On May 24, 2007, Dell started selling models with Ubuntu Linux 7.04 pre-installed: a laptop, a budget computer, and a high-end PC.[[148]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-148)

On June 27, 2007, Dell announced on its Direct2Dell blog that it planned to offer more pre-loaded systems (the new [Dell Inspiron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Inspiron "Dell Inspiron") desktops and laptops). After the [IdeaStorm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_IdeaStorm "Dell IdeaStorm") site supported extending the bundles beyond the US market, Dell later announced more international marketing.[[149]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-149) On August 7, 2007, Dell officially announced that it would offer one notebook and one desktop in the UK, France and Germany with Ubuntu "pre-installed". At [LinuxWorld](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LinuxWorld_Conference_and_Expo "LinuxWorld Conference and Expo") 2007 Dell announced plans to provide [Novell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Novell "Novell")'s [SUSE Linux Enterprise Desktop](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SUSE_Linux_Enterprise_Desktop "SUSE Linux Enterprise Desktop") on selected models in China, "factory-installed".[[150]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-150) On November 30, 2007, Dell reported shipping 40,000 Ubuntu PCs.[[151]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-151) On January 24, 2008, Dell in Germany, Spain, France, and the United Kingdom launched a second laptop, an XPS M1330 with [Ubuntu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ubuntu "Ubuntu") 7.10, for 849 euro or GBP 599 upwards.[[152]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-152) On February 18, 2008, Dell announced that the [Inspiron 1525](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Inspiron_1525 "Dell Inspiron 1525") would have Ubuntu as an optional operating system.[[153]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-153) On February 22, 2008, Dell announced plans to sell Ubuntu in Canada and in [Latin America](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America")[[154]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-154) From September 16, 2008, Dell has shipped both [Dell Ubuntu Netbook Remix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Ubuntu_Netbook_Remix "Dell Ubuntu Netbook Remix") and [Windows XP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Windows_XP "Windows XP") Home versions of the [Inspiron Mini 9](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Inspiron_Mini_9 "Dell Inspiron Mini 9") and the [Inspiron Mini 12](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Inspiron_Mini_12 "Dell Inspiron Mini 12"). As of November 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit) Dell shipped the Inspiron Mini laptops with Ubuntu version 8.04.[[155]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-155)

As of 2021, Dell continues to offer select laptops and workstations with Ubuntu Linux pre-installed, under the "Developer Edition" moniker.[[156]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-156)

## Corporate affairs

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Corporate affairs")]

### Business trends

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Business trends")]

The key trends for Dell are (as of the financial year ending late January/early February):[[157]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-157)[[158]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-158)

|  | Revenue (US$ bn) | Net profit (US$ bn) | Employees (k)[[159]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-159) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2016 | 50.5 | −1.1 | 101 |
| 2017 | 61.5 | −3.6 | 138 |
| 2018 | 79.1 | −2.8 | 145 |
| 2019 | 90.3 | −2.3 | 157 |
| 2020 | 91.9 | 4.6 | 165 |
| 2021 | 86.7 | 2.2 | 158 |
| 2022 | 101 | 4.9 | 133 |
| 2023 | 101 | 2.4 | 133 |
| 2024 | 88.4 | 3.2 | 120 |

### Senior leadership

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Senior leadership")]

#### List of chairmen

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: List of chairmen")]

1.   [Michael Dell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Dell "Michael Dell") (1984– )

#### List of chief executives

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: List of chief executives")]

1.   Michael Dell (1984–2004)
2.   [Kevin Rollins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kevin_Rollins "Kevin Rollins") (2004–2007)
3.   Michael Dell (2007–present); second term

## List of Dell marketing slogans

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: List of Dell marketing slogans")]

*   **Be direct** (1998–2001)
*   **Easy as Dell** (2001–2004)
*   **Get more out of now** (2004–2005)
*   **It's a Dell** (2005–2006)
*   **Dell. Purely You** (2006–2007)
*   **Yours is Here** (2007–2011)
*   **The power to do more** (2011–2023)[[160]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-160)
*   **Welcome to Now** (2023-present)[[161]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-161)

## Acquisitions

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Acquisitions")]

Further information: [List of Dell ownership activities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Dell_ownership_activities "List of Dell ownership activities")

List of companies acquired by Dell Inc. | Company acquired | Date of acquisition | Company notes | References |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Alienware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alienware "Alienware") | 2006 | Manufacturer of high-end PCs for gamers | [[162]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-162)[[163]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-163)[[164]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-AW-164) |
| [EqualLogic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EqualLogic "EqualLogic") | January 28, 2008 | Acquired to gain a foothold in the [iSCSI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISCSI "ISCSI") storage market. Because Dell already had an efficient manufacturing process, integrating EqualLogic's products into the company drove manufacturing prices down | [[165]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-Nov-2007-8-K-165)[[166]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-Jan-2008-8-K-166)[[167]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-167) |
| [Perot Systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perot_Systems "Perot Systems") | 2009 | Perot Systems was a technology services and outsourcing company, mainly active in the health sector, founded by former presidential hopeful [H. Ross Perot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H._Ross_Perot "H. Ross Perot"). The acquired business provided Dell with applications development, systems integration, and strategic consulting services through its operations in the US and 10 other countries. In addition, the acquisition of Perot brought a variety of business process outsourcing services, including claims processing and call center operations. | [[168]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-Sep-2009-8-K-168)[[169]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-169)[[170]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-170) |
| [KACE Networks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KACE_Networks "KACE Networks") | February 10, 2010 | KACE Networks was a leader in systems management appliances. | [[171]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-171) |
| Boomi | November 2, 2010 | Cloud integration leader | [[172]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-htanna-172) |
| [Compellent Technologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compellent_Technologies "Compellent Technologies") | February 2011 | The acquisition extended Dell's storage portfolio. | [[173]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-Mar-2011-10-K-173) |
| [Force10](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Force10 "Force10") networks | August 2011 | By acquiring this company Dell now has the full [Intellectual property](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intellectual_property "Intellectual property") for their networking portfolio, which was lacking on the Dell PowerConnect range as these products are powered by [Broadcom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Broadcom "Broadcom") or [Marvell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marvell_Technology_Group "Marvell Technology Group") IM. | [[174]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-reg10-174) |
| [AppAssure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_AppAssure "Dell AppAssure") Software | February 24, 2012 | Dell acquired the backup and disaster recovery software provider out of Reston, VA. AppAssure delivered 194 percent revenue growth in 2011 and over 3500% growth in the prior three years. AppAssure supported physical servers and VMware, Hyper-V and XenServer. The deal represents the first acquisition since Dell formed its software division under former CA CEO John Swainson. Dell added that it will keep AppAssure's 230 employees and invest in the company. | [[175]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-May-2012-10-Q-175) |
| [SonicWall](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SonicWall "SonicWall") | May 9, 2012 | A company with 130 patents, SonicWall develops security products, and is a network and data security provider. | [[176]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-176)[[177]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-177) |
| [Wyse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wyse "Wyse") | April 2, 2012 | A global market-leader for [thin client](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thin_client "Thin client") systems. | [[175]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-May-2012-10-Q-175)[[178]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-178) |
| Clerity Solutions | April 3, 2012 | Clerity, a company offering services for application (re)hosting, was formed in 1994 and has it headquarters in Chicago. At the time of the take-over approximately 70 people were working for the company. | [[175]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-May-2012-10-Q-175)[[179]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-179) |
| [Quest Software](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quest_Software "Quest Software") | September 28, 2012 |  | [[180]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell-Inc-Jul-2012-8-K-180)[[181]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-181)[[182]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-182)[[183]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-183) |
| [Gale Technologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gale_Technologies "Gale Technologies") | November 16, 2012 | A provider of infrastructure automation products. Gale Technologies was founded in 2008 and is headquartered in Santa Clara, California. | [[184]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-184) |
| [Credant Technologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Credant_Technologies "Credant Technologies") | December 18, 2012 | A provider of storage protection services. Credant is the 19th acquisition in four years, as Dell had spent $13 billion on acquisitions since 2008 and $5 billion in the past year alone. | [[185]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-185)[[186]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-186) |
| [StatSoft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/StatSoft "StatSoft") | March 24, 2014 | A global provider of analytics software, in order to bolster its [big data](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_data "Big data") services offering. | [[187]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-187) |
| [EMC²](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EMC_Corporation "EMC Corporation") | October 12, 2015 | [Storage, virtualization, services, cloud, data center, security and compliance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EMC_Corporation#Products_and_services "EMC Corporation") | [[188]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-188)[[189]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-189)[[190]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-190) |

## Dell facilities

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Dell facilities")]

Dell's headquarters is located in [Round Rock, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Round_Rock,_Texas "Round Rock, Texas").[[191]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-191) As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit) the company employed about 14,000 people in central Texas and was the region's largest private employer,[[192]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-AS-192) which has 2,100,000 square feet (200,000 m 2) of space.[[193]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-GreenHQ-193) As of 1999 almost half of the general fund of the city of Round Rock originated from sales taxes generated from the Dell headquarters.[[194]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-194)

Dell previously had its headquarters in the [Arboretum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arboretum_(Austin,_Texas) "Arboretum (Austin, Texas)") complex in northern Austin, Texas.[[195]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-PopeDis-195)[[196]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-196) In 1989 Dell occupied 127,000 square feet (11,800 m 2) in the Arboretum complex.[[197]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-197) In 1990, Dell had 1,200 employees in its headquarters.[[195]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-PopeDis-195) In 1993, Dell submitted a document to Round Rock officials, titled "Dell Computer Corporate Headquarters, Round Rock, Texas, May 1993 Schematic Design." Despite the filing, during that year the company said that it was not going to move its headquarters.[[198]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-198) In 1994, Dell announced that it was moving most of its employees out of the Arboretum, but that it was going to continue to occupy the top floor of the Arboretum and that the company's official headquarters address would continue to be the Arboretum. The top floor continued to hold Dell's board room, demonstration center, and visitor meeting room. Less than one month prior to August 29, 1994, Dell moved 1,100 customer support and telephone sales employees to Round Rock.[[199]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-199) Dell's lease in the Arboretum had been scheduled to expire in 1994.[[200]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-200)

[![Image 20](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Dell_diamond2.jpg/250px-Dell_diamond2.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dell_diamond2.jpg)

The company sponsors [Dell Diamond](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Diamond "Dell Diamond"), the home stadium of the [Round Rock Express](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Round_Rock_Express "Round Rock Express"), the AAA [minor league baseball](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minor_league_baseball "Minor league baseball") affiliate of the [Texas Rangers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") major league baseball team.

By 1996, Dell was moving its headquarters to Round Rock.[[201]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-HQMoving-201) As of January 1996, 3,500 people still worked at the current Dell headquarters. One building of the Round Rock headquarters, Round Rock 3, had space for 6,400 employees and was scheduled to be completed in November 1996.[[202]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-202) In 1998 Dell announced that it was going to add two buildings to its Round Rock complex, adding 1,600,000 square feet (150,000 m 2) of office space to the complex.[[203]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-203)

In 2000, Dell announced that it would lease 80,000 square feet (7,400 m 2) of space in the [Las Cimas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Las_Cimas "Las Cimas") office complex in [unincorporated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area")[Travis County, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Travis_County,_Texas "Travis County, Texas"), between Austin and [West Lake Hills](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Lake_Hills "West Lake Hills"), to house the company's executive offices and corporate headquarters. 100 senior executives were scheduled to work in the building by the end of 2000.[[204]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-204) In January 2001, the company leased the space in Las Cimas 2, located along [Loop 360](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loop_360 "Loop 360"). Las Cimas 2 housed Dell's executives, the investment operations, and some corporate functions. Dell also had an option for 138,000 square feet (12,800 m 2) of space in Las Cimas 3.[[205]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Subleaselascimas-205) After a slowdown in business required reducing employees and production capacity, Dell decided to sublease its offices in two buildings in the Las Cimas office complex.[[206]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-206) In 2002 Dell announced that it planned to sublease its space to another tenant; the company planned to move its headquarters back to Round Rock once a tenant was secured.[[205]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Subleaselascimas-205) By 2003, Dell moved its headquarters back to Round Rock. It leased all of Las Cimas I and II, with a total of 312,000 square feet (29,000 m 2), for about a seven-year period after 2003. By that year roughly 100,000 square feet (9,300 m 2) of that space was absorbed by new subtenants.[[207]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-207)

In 2008, Dell switched the power sources of the Round Rock headquarters to more environmentally friendly ones, with 60% of the total power coming from [TXU Energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TXU_Energy "TXU Energy") wind farms and 40% coming from the Austin Community Landfill gas-to-energy plant operated by [Waste Management, Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waste_Management,_Inc. "Waste Management, Inc.")[[193]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-GreenHQ-193)

The US and India are the only countries that have all Dell's business functions and provide support globally: research and development, manufacturing, finance, analysis, and customer care.[[208]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-tech.fortune.cnn.com-208) Dell was recognized as "India's Most Desired Brand in 2023", as per TRA's Most Desired Brands report 2023.

### Manufacturing

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Manufacturing")]

From its early beginnings, Dell operated as a pioneer in the "configure to order" approach to manufacturing—delivering individual PCs configured to customer specifications. In contrast, most PC manufacturers in those times delivered large orders to intermediaries on a quarterly basis.[[209]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-209)

To minimize the delay between purchase and delivery, Dell has a general policy of manufacturing its products close to its customers.
