This also allows for implementing a [just-in-time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Just-in-time_(business) "Just-in-time (business)") (JIT) manufacturing approach, which minimizes [inventory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inventory "Inventory") costs. Low inventory is another signature of the Dell business model—a critical consideration in an industry where components depreciate very rapidly.[[210]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-210)

Dell's manufacturing process covers assembly, software installation, functional testing (including "burn-in"), and quality control. Throughout most of the company's history, Dell manufactured desktop machines in-house and contracted out the manufacturing of base notebooks for configuration in-house.[[211]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-211) The company's approach has changed, as cited in the 2006 Annual Report, which states, "We are continuing to expand our use of original design manufacturing partnerships and manufacturing outsourcing relationships." _The Wall Street Journal_ reported in September 2008 that "Dell has approached contract computer manufacturers with offers to sell" their plants.[[212]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-212) By the late 2000s, Dell's "configure to order" approach of manufacturing—delivering individual PCs configured to customer specifications from its US facilities was no longer as efficient or competitive with high-volume Asian contract manufacturers as PCs became powerful low-cost commodities.[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-statesman1-65)

Assembly of desktop computers for the North American market formerly took place at Dell plants in Austin, Texas, (original location) and [Lebanon, Tennessee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lebanon,_Tennessee "Lebanon, Tennessee"), (opened in 1999), which were closed in 2008 and early 2009, respectively. The plant in [Winston-Salem, North Carolina](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Winston-Salem,_North_Carolina "Winston-Salem, North Carolina"), opened in 2005 but ceased operations in November 2010.[[69]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-theregister.co.uk-69)[[70]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-bizjournals.com-70) Most of the work that used to take place in Dell's US plants was transferred to contract manufacturers in Asia and Mexico, or some of Dell's own factories overseas. The [Miami, Florida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miami,_Florida "Miami, Florida"), facility of its Alienware subsidiary remains in operation, while Dell continues to produce its servers (its most profitable products) in Austin, Texas.[[65]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-statesman1-65)

Dell assembled computers for the [EMEA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Europe,_the_Middle_East_and_Africa "Europe, the Middle East and Africa") market at the [Limerick](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Limerick "Limerick") facility in the Republic of Ireland, and once employed about 4,500 people in that country. Dell began manufacturing in Limerick in 1991 and went on to become Ireland's largest exporter of goods and its second-largest company and foreign investor. On January 8, 2009, Dell announced that it would move all Dell manufacturing in Limerick to Dell's new plant in the Polish city of [Łódź](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA "Łódź") by January 2010.[[213]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-213)[European Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/European_Union "European Union") officials said they would investigate a €52.7million aid package the Polish government used to attract Dell away from Ireland.[[214]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-214) European Manufacturing Facility 1 (EMF1, opened in 1990) and EMF3 form part of the [Raheen Industrial Estate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raheen_Industrial_Estate "Raheen Industrial Estate") near Limerick. Dell Inc. has consolidated production into EMF3 (EMF1 now[_[when?](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual\_of\_Style/Dates\_and\_numbers#Chronological\_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")_] contains only offices).[[215]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-215) Subsidies from the Polish government did keep Dell for a long time.[[216]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-216) After ending assembly in the Limerick plant the [Cherrywood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cherrywood,_Dublin "Cherrywood, Dublin") Technology Campus in Dublin was the largest Dell office in the republic with over 1200 people in sales (mainly UK & Ireland), support (enterprise support for EMEA) and research and development for cloud computing, but no more manufacturing except[[217]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ida-217) Dell's Alienware subsidiary, which manufactures PCs in an Athlone, Ireland, plant. Whether this facility will remain in Ireland is not certain.[[218]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-218) Dell started production at EMF4 in Łódź, Poland, in late 2007.[[219]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-219)

Dell moved desktop, notebook and PowerEdge server manufacturing for the South American market from the [Eldorado do Sul](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eldorado_do_Sul,_Brazil "Eldorado do Sul, Brazil") plant opened in 1999, to a new plant in [Hortolândia, Brazil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hortol%C3%A2ndia "Hortolândia"), in 2007.[[220]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-220)

## Products

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Products")]

### Scope and brands

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Scope and brands")]

[![Image 21](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Dell_wiki.JPG/250px-Dell_wiki.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Dell_wiki.JPG)

Dell's 2007–2011 tagline "Yours is Here", as seen at their [Mall of Asia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SM_Mall_of_Asia "SM Mall of Asia") branch in [Pasay](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pasay "Pasay"), Philippines

The corporation markets specific brand names to different [market segments](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Market_segment "Market segment").

Its Business/Corporate class includes:

*   [OptiPlex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_OptiPlex "Dell OptiPlex") (office [desktop computer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer") systems)
*   [Dimension](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Dimension "Dell Dimension") (home desktop computer systems)
*   [Vostro](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Vostro "Dell Vostro") (office/small business desktop and [notebook](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laptop "Laptop") systems)
*   [n Series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_n_Series "Dell n Series") (desktop and notebook computers shipped with [Linux](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux "Linux") or [FreeDOS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FreeDOS "FreeDOS") installed)
*   [Latitude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Latitude "Dell Latitude") (business-focused notebooks)
*   [Precision](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Precision "Dell Precision") ([workstation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_workstation "Computer workstation") systems and high-performance "Mobile Workstation" notebooks),[[221]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-221)
*   [PowerEdge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_PowerEdge "Dell PowerEdge") (business servers)
*   [PowerVault](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_PowerVault "Dell PowerVault") (direct-attach and [network-attached storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network-attached_storage "Network-attached storage"))
*   [Force10](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Force10 "Force10") ([network switches](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_switch "Network switch"))
*   [PowerConnect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_PowerConnect "Dell PowerConnect") ([network switches](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_switch "Network switch"))
*   [Dell Compellent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Compellent "Dell Compellent") ([storage area networks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Storage_area_network "Storage area network"))
*   [EqualLogic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EqualLogic "EqualLogic") (enterprise class [iSCSI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISCSI "ISCSI")[SANs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Storage_area_network "Storage area network"))
*   [Dell EMR](https://web.archive.org/web/20130123095422/http://content.dell.com/us/en/healthcare/healthcare-electronic-medical-records.aspx) (electronic medical records)

Dell's Home Office/Consumer class includes:

*   [XPS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_XPS "Dell XPS") (high-end desktop and notebook computers)
*   [Alienware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alienware "Alienware") (high-performance gaming systems)

Dell's Peripherals class includes [USB keydrives](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keydrive "Keydrive"), [LCD televisions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LCD_television "LCD television"), and [printers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Printer_(computing) "Printer (computing)"); Dell monitors includes [LCD TVs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LCD "LCD"), [plasma TVs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasma_display "Plasma display") and [projectors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Video_projector "Video projector") for [HDTV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HDTV "HDTV") and [monitors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_display "Computer display"). Dell UltraSharp is further a high-end brand of monitors.

Dell service and support brands include the _Dell Solution Station_ (extended domestic support services, previously "Dell on Call"), _[Dell Support Center](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell\_Support\_Center "Dell Support Center")_ (extended support services abroad), _Dell Business Support_ (a commercial service-contract that provides an industry-certified technician with a lower call-volume than in normal queues), _Dell Everdream Desktop Management_ ("[Software as a service](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_as_a_service "Software as a service")" [remote-desktop management](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Remote-desktop_management "Remote-desktop management"), originally a SaaS company founded by [Elon Musk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elon_Musk "Elon Musk")'s cousin, [Lyndon Rive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lyndon_Rive "Lyndon Rive"), which Dell bought in 2007[[222]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-222)), and _Your Tech Team_ (a support-queue available to home users who purchased their systems either through Dell's website or through Dell phone-centers).

Discontinued products and brands include [Axim](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Axim "Dell Axim") ([PDA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Personal_digital_assistant "Personal digital assistant"); discontinued April 9, 2007),[[223]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-223)[Dimension](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Dimension "Dell Dimension") (home and small office desktop computers; discontinued July 2007), [Dell Digital Jukebox](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Digital_Jukebox "Dell Digital Jukebox") (MP3 player; discontinued August 2006), [G Series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_G_Series "Dell G Series") (high/medium-performance gaming laptops),[[224]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-224)[Inspiron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Inspiron "Dell Inspiron") (medium-range desktop and notebook computers),[[225]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-225)[[226]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-226)[[227]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-227)[[228]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-228)[[229]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-cnetdellname-229)[[230]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-mashabledellname-230) Dell PowerApp (application-based servers), Dell Optiplex (desktop and tower computers previously supported to run server and desktop operating systems), Dell Unix (an [SVR4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UNIX_System_V "UNIX System V")-based Unix operating system for its Dell-branded PCs and workstations; discontinued in 1993), [Venue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_Venue "Dell Venue") (Tablets Android / Windows),[[231]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-231) and Dell Mobile Connect(Windows Mobile application; discontinued July 31, 2022).[[232]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-232)

## Security

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Security")]

### Self-signed root certificate

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Self-signed root certificate")]

In November 2015, it emerged that several Dell computers had shipped with an identical pre-installed [root certificate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Root_certificate "Root certificate") known as "eDellRoot".[[233]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ars-selfsigned-233) This raised such security risks as attackers impersonating [HTTPS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTPS "HTTPS")-protected websites such as [Google](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Google "Google") and [Bank of America](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America") and malware being signed with the certificate to bypass Microsoft software filtering.[[233]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ars-selfsigned-233) Dell apologized and offered a removal tool.[[234]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ars-removal-234)

### Dell Foundation Services

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Dell Foundation Services")]

Also in November 2015, a researcher discovered that customers with diagnostic program Dell Foundation Services could be digitally tracked using the unique service tag number assigned to them by the program.[[235]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ars-dfs-235) This was possible even if a customer enabled [private browsing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Private_browsing "Private browsing") and deleted their [browser cookies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Browser_cookie "Browser cookie").[[235]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ars-dfs-235)_[Ars Technica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ars\_Technica "Ars Technica")_ recommended that Dell customers uninstall the program until the issue was addressed.[[235]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-ars-dfs-235)

## Commercial aspects

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Commercial aspects")]

### Marketing

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Marketing")]

Dell advertisements have appeared in several types of media including television, the Internet, magazines, [catalogs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mail-order_catalog "Mail-order catalog"), and newspapers. Some of Dell Inc's marketing strategies include lowering prices at all times of the year, free bonus products (such as Dell printers), and free shipping to encourage more sales and stave off competitors. In 2006, Dell cut its prices in an effort to maintain its 19.2% market share. This also cut profit margins by more than half, from 8.7 to 4.3 percent. To maintain its low prices, Dell continues to accept most purchases of its products via the Internet and through the telephone network, and to move its customer-care division to India and [El Salvador](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador").[[236]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-236)

A popular United States television and print ad campaign in the early 2000s featured the actor [Ben Curtis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ben_Curtis_(actor) "Ben Curtis (actor)") playing the part of "Steven", a lightly mischievous blond-haired youth who came to the assistance of bereft computer purchasers. Each television advertisement usually ended with Steven's catch-phrase: "Dude, you're gettin' a Dell!"[[237]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-SlateDell-237)

A subsequent advertising campaign featured [interns](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intern "Intern") at Dell headquarters (with Curtis' character appearing in a small cameo at the end of one of the first commercials in this particular campaign).

In 2007, Dell switched advertising agencies in the US from [BBDO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BBDO "BBDO") to [Working Mother Media](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_Mother "Working Mother"). In July 2007, Dell released new advertising created by Working Mother to support the Inspiron and XPS lines. The ads featured music from the [Flaming Lips](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Flaming_Lips "The Flaming Lips") and [Devo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Devo "Devo") who re-formed especially to record the song in the ad "Work it Out". Also in 2007, Dell began using the slogan "Yours is here" to say that it customizes computers to fit customers' requirements.[[238]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-engadget-238)

In 2011, Dell began hosting an annual conference titled "Dell World" at the Austin Convention Center in Austin, Texas. The event featured new technology and services provided by Dell and Dell's partners.[[239]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-239)[[240]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-240) After Dell and EMC merged in 2016, their annual conferences were combined into "Dell EMC World", hosted in Las Vegas, Nevada. It was renamed "Dell Technologies World" in 2018.[[241]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-241)[[242]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-242)

#### Dell partner program

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Dell partner program")]

In late 2007, Dell Inc. announced that it planned to expand its program to [value-added resellers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Value-added_reseller "Value-added reseller") (VARs), giving it the official name of "Dell Partner Direct" and a new Website.[[243]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-243)

In 2013, Dell India started an ecommerce website[[244]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-244) with its Dell Partner GNG Electronics Pvt Ltd[[245]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-245) termed as Dell Express Ship Affiliate (DESA). The main objective was to reduce the delivery time. Customers who visited the Dell India official site and selected the option to buy online were redirected to the Dell affiliate website.[[208]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-tech.fortune.cnn.com-208)

#### Criticisms of marketing of laptop security

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Criticisms of marketing of laptop security")]

In 2008, Dell received press coverage over its claim of having the world's most secure laptops, specifically, its Latitude D630 and Latitude D830.[[246]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-246) At Lenovo's request, the (US) National Advertising Division (NAD) evaluated the claim, and reported that Dell did not have enough evidence to support it.[[247]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-247)

### Retail

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Retail")]

Dell first opened their retail stores in India.[[208]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-tech.fortune.cnn.com-208)

#### United States

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: United States")]

In the early 1990s, Dell sold its products through [Best Buy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Best_Buy "Best Buy"), [Costco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Costco "Costco") and [Sam's Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sam%27s_Club "Sam's Club") stores in the United States. Dell stopped this practice in 1994, citing low profit margins on the business, exclusively distributing through a direct-sales model for the next decade. In 2003, Dell briefly sold products in [Sears](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sears "Sears") stores in the US. In 2007, Dell started shipping its products to major retailers in the US once again, starting with [Sam's Club](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sam%27s_Club "Sam's Club") and [Wal-Mart](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Walmart "Walmart"). [Staples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Staples_Inc. "Staples Inc."), the largest office-supply retailer in the US, and Best Buy, the largest electronics retailer in the US, became Dell retail partners later that same year.

##### Kiosks

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Kiosks")]

Starting in 2002, Dell opened [kiosk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kiosk "Kiosk") locations in the United States to allow customers to examine products before buying them directly from the company. Starting in 2005, Dell expanded [kiosk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kiosk "Kiosk") locations to include shopping malls across Australia, Canada, Singapore and Hong Kong. On January 30, 2008, Dell announced it would shut down all 140 kiosks in the US due to expansion into retail stores.[[248]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-248) By June 3, 2010, Dell had also shut down all of its mall kiosks in Australia.[[249]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-249)

#### Retail stores

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Retail stores")]

As of the end of February 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit), Dell products shipped to one of the largest office supply retailers in Canada, [Staples Business Depot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Staples_Business_Depot "Staples Business Depot"). In April 2008, [Future Shop](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Future_Shop "Future Shop") and [Best Buy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Best_Buy "Best Buy") began carrying a subset of Dell products, such as certain desktops, laptops, printers, and monitors.

Since some shoppers in certain markets show reluctance to purchase technological products through the phone or the Internet, Dell has looked into opening retail operations in some countries in Central Europe and Russia. In April 2007, Dell opened a retail store in [Budapest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"). In October of the same year, Dell opened a retail store in Moscow.

In the UK, [HMV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HMV "HMV")'s flagship [Trocadero](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trocadero_(London) "Trocadero (London)") store has sold Dell XPS PCs since December 2007. From January 2008 the UK stores of [DSGi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DSG_International_(retailer) "DSG International (retailer)") have sold Dell products (in particular, through [Currys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Currys "Currys") and [PC World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PC_World_(retailer) "PC World (retailer)") stores). As of 2008, the large supermarket chain [Tesco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tesco "Tesco") has sold Dell laptops and desktops in outlets throughout the UK.

In May 2008, Dell reached an agreement with the office supply chain, [Officeworks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Officeworks "Officeworks") (part of [Coles Group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coles_Group "Coles Group")), to stock a few modified models in the Inspiron desktop and notebook range. These models have slightly different model numbers, but almost replicate the ones available from the Dell Store. Dell continued its retail push in the Australian market with its partnership with Harris Technology (another part of Coles Group) in November of the same year. In addition, Dell expanded its retail distributions in Australia through an agreement with the discount electrical retailer, [The Good Guys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Good_Guys_(Australasia) "The Good Guys (Australasia)"), known for "Slashing Prices". Dell agreed to distribute a variety of makes of both desktops and notebooks, including [Studio](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Studio "Studio") and [XPS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell_XPS "Dell XPS") systems in late 2008. Dell and [Dick Smith Electronics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dick_Smith_Electronics "Dick Smith Electronics") (owned by [Woolworths Limited](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Woolworths_Limited "Woolworths Limited")) reached an agreement to expand within Dick Smith's 400 stores throughout Australia and New Zealand in May 2009 (1 year since Officeworks—owned by Coles Group—reached a deal). The retailer has agreed to distribute a variety of [Inspiron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inspiron "Inspiron") and [Studio](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Studio "Studio") notebooks, with minimal Studio desktops from the Dell range. As of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit), Dell continues to run and operate its various kiosks in 18 shopping centers throughout Australia. On March 31, 2010, Dell announced to Australian Kiosk employees that they were shutting down the Australian/New Zealand Dell kiosk program.

In Germany, Dell is selling selected smartphones and notebooks via [Media Markt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Media_Markt "Media Markt") and Saturn, as well as some shopping websites.[[250]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-250)

### Competition

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Competition")]

Dell's major competitors include [Lenovo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo"), [Hewlett-Packard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard") (HP), [Hasee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hasee "Hasee"), [Acer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acer_Inc. "Acer Inc."), [Fujitsu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fujitsu "Fujitsu"), [Toshiba](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toshiba "Toshiba"), [Gateway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gateway,_Inc. "Gateway, Inc."), [Sony](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sony "Sony"), [Asus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asus "Asus"), [MSI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Micro-Star_International "Micro-Star International"), [Panasonic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic"), [Samsung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") and [Apple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."). Dell and its subsidiary, Alienware, compete in the enthusiast market against AVADirect, [Falcon Northwest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Falcon_Northwest "Falcon Northwest"), [VoodooPC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VoodooPC "VoodooPC") (a subsidiary of HP), and other manufacturers. In the second quarter of 2006, Dell had between 18% and 19% share of the worldwide personal computer market, compared to HP with roughly 15%.

In late 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit), Dell lost its lead in the PC business to Hewlett-Packard. Both [Gartner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gartner "Gartner") and [IDC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Data_Corporation "International Data Corporation") estimated that in the third quarter of 2006, HP shipped more units[[251]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-251) worldwide than Dell did. Dell's 3.6% growth paled in comparison to HP's 15% growth during the same period. The problem got worse in the fourth quarter, when Gartner estimated[[252]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-252) that Dell PC shipments declined 8.9% (versus HP's 23.9% growth). As a result, at the end of 2006 Dell's overall PC market share stood at 13.9% (versus HP's 17.4%).

IDC reported that Dell lost more server market share than any of the top four competitors in that arena. IDC's Q4 2006 estimates show Dell's share of the server market at 8.1%, down from 9.5% in the previous year. This represents an 8.8% loss year-over-year, primarily to competitors EMC and IBM. As of 2025, Dell is the third-largest PC manufacturer after Lenovo and HP Inc.[[253]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-253)

### Partnership with EMC

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Partnership with EMC")]

In 2001, Dell and EMC entered into a partnership whereby both companies jointly design products, and Dell provided support for certain EMC products including midrange storage systems, such as [fibre channel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibre_channel "Fibre channel") and [iSCSI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISCSI "ISCSI")[storage area networks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Storage_area_network "Storage area network"). The relationship also promotes and sells OEM versions of backup, recovery, replication and archiving software.[[254]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-254) On December 9, 2008, Dell and EMC announced the multi-year extension, through 2013, of the strategic partnership with EMC. In addition, Dell expanded its product lineup by adding the EMC Celerra NX4 storage system to the portfolio of Dell/EMC family of networked storage systems and partnered on a new line of [data deduplication](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_deduplication "Data deduplication") products as part of its TierDisk family of [data storage devices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Data_storage_device "Data storage device").[[255]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-255)

On October 17, 2011, Dell discontinued reselling all EMC storage products, ending the partnership 2 years early.[[256]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-256)[[257]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-257) Later Dell would acquire and merge with EMC in the largest tech merger to date.

## Environmental record

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Environmental record")]

Dell committed to reducing greenhouse gas emissions from its global activities by 40% by 2015, with the 2008 fiscal year as the baseline year.[[258]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-258) It is listed in [Greenpeace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greenpeace "Greenpeace")'s Guide to Greener Electronics that scores leading electronics manufacturers according to their policies on sustainability, [climate and energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_and_energy "Climate and energy") and how green their products are. In November 2011, Dell ranked 2nd out of 15 listed electronics makers (increasing its score to 5.1 from 4.9, which it gained in the previous ranking from October 2010).[[259]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-259)

Dell was the first company to publicly state a timeline for the elimination of toxic polyvinyl chloride (PVC) and brominated flame retardants (BFRs), which it planned to phase out by the end of 2009. It revised this commitment and now aims to remove toxics by the end of 2011 but only in its computing products.[[260]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-260) In March 2010, Greenpeace activists protested at Dell offices in Bangalore, Amsterdam and Copenhagen calling for Dell's founder and CEO Michael Dell to "drop the toxics" and claiming that Dell's aspiration to be 'the greenest technology company on the planet'[[261]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-261) was "hypocritical".[[262]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-262) Dell has launched its first products completely free of PVC and BFRs with the G-Series monitors (G2210 and G2410) in 2009.[[263]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-263)

In its 2012 report on progress relating to [conflict minerals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conflict_minerals "Conflict minerals"), the [Enough Project](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enough_Project "Enough Project") rated Dell the eighth-highest of 24 consumer electronics companies.[[264]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-EnoughProject2012-264)

In its enterprise product line, Dell has integrated high-efficiency power supply units (PSUs) to reduce energy consumption. As of 2025, numerous Dell server components have achieved the [80 PLUS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/80_Plus "80 Plus") Titanium certification, the highest energy efficiency tier for standard power delivery, ensuring a minimum of 96% efficiency at 50% load.[[265]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-265)

### Green initiatives

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Green initiatives")]

Dell became the first company in the [information technology industry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Information_technology_industry "Information technology industry") to establish a product-[recycling](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Recycling "Recycling") goal (in 2004) and completed the implementation of its global consumer recycling-program in 2006.[[266]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-266) On February 6, 2007, the National Recycling Coalition awarded Dell its "Recycling Works" award for efforts to promote producer responsibility.[[267]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-National_Recycling_Coalition-267) On July 19, 2007, Dell announced that it had exceeded targets in working to achieve a multi-year goal of recovering 275 million pounds of computer equipment by 2009. The company reported the recovery of 78 million pounds (nearly 40,000 tons) of IT equipment from customers in 2006, a 93-percent increase over 2005; and 12.4% of the equipment Dell sold seven years earlier.[[268]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-Dell_Inc.-268)

On June 5, 2007, Dell set a goal of becoming the greenest technology company on Earth for the long term.[[269]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-269) The company launched a [zero-carbon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zero-carbon "Zero-carbon") initiative that includes:

1.   reducing Dell's [carbon intensity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_intensity "Carbon intensity") by 15 percent by 2012
2.   requiring primary suppliers to report carbon emissions data during quarterly business reviews
3.   partnering with customers to build the "greenest PC on the planet"
4.   expanding the company's carbon-offsetting program, "Plant a Tree for Me"

Dell reports its environmental performance in an annual [Corporate Social Responsibility](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corporate_Social_Responsibility "Corporate Social Responsibility") (CSR) Report that follows the [Global Reporting Initiative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_Reporting_Initiative "Global Reporting Initiative") (GRI) protocol. Dell's 2008 CSR report ranked as "Application Level B" as "checked by GRI".[[270]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-270)

The company aims to reduce its external environmental impact through an energy-efficient evolution of products, and also reduce its direct operational impact through energy-efficiency programs.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

## Criticism

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Dell&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Criticism")]

See also: [Lawsuits involving Dell Inc.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lawsuits_involving_Dell_Inc. "Lawsuits involving Dell Inc.")

In the 1990s, Dell switched from using primarily [ATX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ATX "ATX")[motherboards](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Motherboard "Motherboard") and [PSU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_power_supply "Computer power supply") to using boards and power supplies with mechanically identical but differently wired connectors. This meant customers wishing to upgrade their hardware would have to replace parts with scarce Dell-compatible parts instead of commonly available parts. While motherboard power connections reverted to the industry standard in 2003, Dell remains secretive about their motherboard pin-outs for peripherals (such as MMC readers and power on/off switches and LEDs).[[271]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-271)[[272]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-272)

In 2005, complaints about Dell more than doubled to 1,533, after earnings grew 52% that year.[[273]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-273)

In 2006, Dell acknowledged that it had problems with customer service. Issues included call transfers[[274]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-274) of more than 45% of calls and long wait times. Dell's [blog](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blog "Blog") detailed the response: "We're spending more than a $100 million—and a lot of blood, sweat, and tears of talented people—to fix this."[[275]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-275) Later in the year, the company increased its spending on customer service to $150 million.[[276]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-276) Since 2018, Dell has seen significant increase in consumer satisfaction. Moreover, their customer service has been praised for its prompt and accurate answers to most questions, especially those directed to their social media support.[[277]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-277)[[278]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-278)

On August 17, 2007, Dell Inc. announced that after an internal investigation into its accounting practices it would restate and reduce earnings from 2003 through to the first quarter of 2007 by a total amount of between $50 million and $150 million, or 2 cents to 7 cents per share.[[279]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-279) The investigation, begun in November 2006, resulted from concerns raised by the [U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U.S._Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission") over some documents and information that Dell Inc. had submitted.[[280]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-280) It was alleged that Dell had not disclosed large exclusivity payments received from [Intel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intel "Intel") for agreeing not to buy processors from rival manufacturer [AMD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AMD "AMD"). In 2010 Dell finally paid $100 million (equivalent to $140,000,000 in 2024) to settle the SEC's charges of fraud. Michael Dell and other executives also paid penalties and suffered other sanctions, without admitting or denying the charges.[[281]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-281)

In July 2009, Dell apologized after drawing the ire of the Taiwanese Consumer Protection Commission for twice refusing to honor a flood of orders against unusually low prices offered on its Taiwanese website. In the first instance, Dell offered a 19" LCD panel for $15. In the second instance, Dell offered its Latitude E4300 notebook at NT$18,558 (US$580), 70% lower than the usual price of NT$60,900 (US$1900). Concerning the E4300, rather than honor the discount taking a significant loss, the firm withdrew orders and offered a voucher of up to NT$20,000 (US$625) a customer in compensation. The consumer rights authorities in Taiwan fined Dell NT$1 million (US$31250) for customer rights infringements. Many consumers sued the firm for unfair compensation. A court in southern Taiwan ordered the firm to deliver 18 laptops and 76 flat-panel monitors to 31 consumers for NT$490,000 (US$15,120), less than a third of the normal price.[[282]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-282) The court said the event could hardly be regarded as mistakes, as the prestigious firm said the company mispriced its products twice on its Taiwanese website within 3 weeks.[[283]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-283)

After Michael Dell made a $24.4 billion buyout bid in August 2013 (equivalent to $32,310,000,000 in 2024), activist shareholder [Carl Icahn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carl_Icahn "Carl Icahn") sued the company and its board in an attempt to derail the bid and promote his own forthcoming offer.[[284]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-284)

In 2020, the [Australian Strategic Policy Institute](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australian_Strategic_Policy_Institute "Australian Strategic Policy Institute") accused at least 82 major brands, including Dell, of being connected to forced [Uyghur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs") labor in [Xinjiang](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang").[[285]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_note-285)

Archived from [the original](http://director.co.uk/MAGAZINE/2009/4%20April/Michael_Dell_62_9.html) on August 15, 2014.
46.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_ref-46)**Michael Dell had a risk-averse management style and he openly mocked rivals that spent on [R&D](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") and acquisitions, though by the late 2000s this may have contributed to Dell missing market shifts like mobile phones and tablet computers.[[1]](https://web.archive.org/web/20130124121741/http://www.businessweek.com/articles/2013-01-24/boeings-787-dreamliner-and-the-decline-of-innovation#p2)
47.   **[^](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dell#cite_ref-47)**["Dell Learns to Listen"](https://web.archive.org/web/20150924150128/http://www.businessweek.com/printer/articles/294370-dell-learns-to-listen?type=old_article). _Bloomberg BusinessWeek_. October 17, 2007. Archived from [the original](http://www.businessweek.com/printer/articles/294370-dell-learns-to-listen?type=old_article) on September 24, 2015. Retrieved January 9, 2014.

The Ottawa centre is closing because Dell can't justify paying $18 per hour with the Canadian and American currencies at parity. The relatively high pay, benefits and training opportunities separated Dell from other call centres ...

*   [Dell as the seventh-most-admired computer company in the USA](https://money.cnn.com/magazines/fortune/mostadmired/industries/industry_114.html), [eighth overall](https://money.cnn.com/magazines/fortune/mostadmired/), [and seventh worldwide](https://money.cnn.com/magazines/fortune/globalmostadmired/top50/). _[Fortune](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fortune\_(magazine) "Fortune (magazine)")_, "Most Admired Companies 2006".
*   [BBC News](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BBC_News "BBC News"), August 21, 2003, ["Dell makes grab for market share"](https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/business/3169407.stm)
*   _[USA Today](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/USA\_Today "USA Today")_, January 20, 2001, ["Dell business model turns to muscle as rivals struggle"](https://www.usatoday.com/money/industries/technology/2003-01-19-Dell-cover_x.htm)

"Acer Inc.") * [Apple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc. "HP Inc.") * [Lenovo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo") * [LG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LG_Electronics "LG Electronics") * [Microsoft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") * [MSI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Micro-Star_International "Micro-Star International") * [NEC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NEC "NEC") * [Panasonic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic") * [Razer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Razer_Inc. "Razer Inc.") * [Samsung](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics") * [Sharp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sharp_Corporation "Sharp Corporation") ([Dynabook](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dynabook_Inc. "Dynabook Inc.")) * [Vaio](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vaio "Vaio") | Servers only | * [Cisco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cisco "Cisco") * [EMC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EMC_Corporation "EMC Corporation") * [HPE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hewlett_Packard_Enterprise "Hewlett Packard Enterprise") * [IBM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM "IBM") * [Inspur](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inspur "Inspur") * [NetApp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NetApp "NetApp") * [Oracle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation") | | --- | | | [Mainframes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mainframe_computer "Mainframe computer") | * [Fujitsu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fujitsu "Fujitsu") * [HPE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hewlett_Packard_Enterprise "Hewlett Packard Enterprise") * [IBM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM "IBM") | | See also[Largest IT companies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_largest_information_technology_companies_by_revenue "List of largest information technology companies by revenue")[Computer hardware manufacturers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_computer_hardware_manufacturers "List of computer hardware manufacturers")[Home computer hardware companies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:Home_computer_hardware_companies "Category:Home computer hardware companies")[Server hardware](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:Server_hardware "Category:Server hardware")[Mainframe computers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:Mainframe_computers "Category:Mainframe computers") | |
